body_txt: All stories need a structure, but some stories have a schedule . The beats all land in the same place; the characters all serve their usual roles. The good guys do good things; the bad guys do bad things.  Just as we reach the highest pitch in the story, the good guys confront the bad guys.  The bad guys lose and then die, go to prison, or agree to stop doing bad things. Everyone laughs, roll credits. Any given summary of the plot is a perfect match for this week's episode and last week's episode, except for a few details about how the bad guys were bad, how the good guys were good, and what happened to the bad guys afterward. The show's big confrontation reaches its peak just before the third commercial break and wraps up after: the Once an Episode events can often be predicted down to the minute. Pretty dull , huh? Then why is almost every one of the top-rated shows on TV like this? Why do romance and mystery novels outsell works from other genres, even though every book is basically the same story ? Why do some even receive critical acclaim and awards? Because the people who read/view them are freed up from discerning the structure and can concentrate on the language and the details . As the Observation on Originality ... er, observes, it is difficult to have quality and novelty, but layering new parts over a known foundation can be a solid compromise. Everyone already knows the basic shape of the story, so writer and audience can turn all their attention to the content. Consider the Monster of the Week series: yes, we all go in knowing that a single, unique villain will turn out to be behind everything, but we still get the fun of wondering what kind of villain, what evil it will do, how the heroes will discover it, and how they finally defeat it. On the other hand, those who do find their entertainment in piecing out the shape of the story will almost certainly be left disappointed, since this type of writing is completely incongruent with Genre Busting . By way of consolation, they are given everything that is not Strictly Formula. The difficulty is that every show will exhaust the potential of the formula eventually. And attempts to shake it up will provoke cries of " They Changed It, Now It Sucks! " Further, even when a show genuinely breaks new ground , it can retroactively acquire a reputation for being formulaic because so many other creators were inspired by it and made works which followed the same pattern. See also Status Quo Is God and Same Story, Different Names . Layout of a Season is the season-wide version of this. Just watch out. Sometimes the formula isn't there, but just perceived by the general public. A classic case is how every protagonist in H. P. Lovecraft 's work dies or goes insane in the end. Except that rarely happens. Then the formula becomes Common Knowledge , without ever really existing .
